title: Why Privacy Matters in the Age of Surveillance Capitalism
excerpt: Your data is the product. Your attention is the currency. Here's why building privacy-first tools isn't optional — it's survival.
content:
## The Surveillance Economy

Every click, every scroll, every pause — it's all being tracked, analyzed, and monetized. The modern internet wasn't built for users. It was built for advertisers.

We've normalized the idea that "free" services are acceptable when the payment is our personal data. But the cost is far greater than most realize.

## The Real Cost of Free

When you use a free email service, you're not the customer — you're the product. Your conversations are scanned, your contacts are mapped, your behavior patterns are sold to the highest bidder.

> "If you're not paying for the product, you are the product."

This isn't just a privacy concern. It's a **power asymmetry**. Companies know more about you than you know about yourself.

## Building the Alternative

At NullShift, we build tools that flip this model:

- **Zero-knowledge architecture**: We can't see your data, even if we wanted to
- **End-to-end encryption**: Your keys, your data
- **No metadata logging**: We don't track what we don't need
- **Open source**: Trust through transparency, not promises

## The Path Forward

Privacy isn't a feature. It's a fundamental right. Every line of code we write starts with one question: *does this protect the user?*

The tools exist. The cryptography is proven. What we need now is the will to build differently.

```
nullshift@labs$ echo "Privacy is not a luxury."
Privacy is not a luxury.
nullshift@labs$ _
```

The future is encrypted. Build accordingly.

title: Zero-Knowledge Proofs: Proving Everything, Revealing Nothing
excerpt: A technical deep-dive into how ZK proofs work, why they matter for blockchain privacy, and how we're using them at NullShift.
content:
## What Are Zero-Knowledge Proofs?

Imagine proving you know a password without ever revealing the password itself. That's the essence of zero-knowledge proofs (ZKPs) — a cryptographic method where a prover can convince a verifier that a statement is true without revealing any information beyond the validity of the statement.

## The Three Properties

Every ZK proof must satisfy:

1. **Completeness**: If the statement is true, an honest verifier will be convinced
2. **Soundness**: If the statement is false, no cheating prover can convince the verifier
3. **Zero-Knowledge**: The verifier learns nothing beyond the truth of the statement

## SNARKs vs STARKs

### SNARKs (Succinct Non-interactive Arguments of Knowledge)
- Require a trusted setup ceremony
- Smaller proof sizes (~200 bytes)
- Faster verification time
- Used in Zcash, Tornado Cash

### STARKs (Scalable Transparent Arguments of Knowledge)
- No trusted setup needed
- Larger proof sizes (~50-100KB)
- Post-quantum secure
- Used in StarkNet, zkSync

## Real-World Applications

- **Private transactions**: Prove you have sufficient funds without revealing your balance
- **Identity verification**: Prove you're over 18 without revealing your birthdate
- **Voting**: Prove you voted without revealing your choice
- **Supply chain**: Prove compliance without revealing trade secrets

## Our Implementation

The NullShift zkProof SDK provides:

```
// Define a circuit
circuit ProveBalance {
  private input balance;
  private input threshold;
  public output valid;

  valid = balance >= threshold;
}
```

We're making ZK accessible to developers who want to build privacy-preserving applications without a PhD in cryptography.

## The Future

ZK proofs are the foundation of the next internet — one where you can prove anything without revealing everything.

title: Autonomous AI Agents in DeFi: Architecture of Our Trading System
excerpt: How we built a multi-agent system that monitors markets, executes trades, and manages risk — all without human intervention.
content:
## The Multi-Agent Architecture

Our DeFi trading system isn't a single bot — it's a network of specialized agents, each with a distinct role:

- **Market Scanner**: Ingests data from 50+ sources
- **Trading Agent**: Executes strategies across DEX protocols  
- **Alert Bot**: Real-time notifications via encrypted channels
- **Portfolio Tracker**: Cross-chain PnL and risk monitoring

## Why Multi-Agent?

Single-agent systems have a fundamental flaw: they try to do everything. This leads to complexity, fragility, and poor performance.

Our approach separates concerns:

> Each agent does one thing exceptionally well, and communicates with others through well-defined interfaces.

## The Signal Pipeline

```
Market Data → Scanner → Signal Queue → Strategy Engine → Execution
     ↓                        ↓                            ↓
  Alert Bot            Risk Check                  Portfolio Update
```

## Risk Management

Every trade passes through multiple risk gates:

1. **Position sizing**: Never risk more than 2% of portfolio per trade
2. **Correlation check**: Avoid concentrated exposure
3. **Volatility filter**: Reduce size in high-volatility regimes
4. **Circuit breaker**: Halt all trading if drawdown exceeds threshold

## Performance

Over the last 30 days:
- Win rate: 67.8%
- Sharpe ratio: 2.1
- Max drawdown: -4.2%
- Avg latency: < 200ms

## Lessons Learned

- **Latency matters**: In DeFi, milliseconds can mean the difference between profit and loss
- **Redundancy is key**: Always have fallback RPC endpoints
- **Monitor everything**: If you can't measure it, you can't improve it

The agents never sleep. Neither does the market.

title: The Case for Decentralized Identity on Blockchain
excerpt: Why centralized identity systems are broken, and how blockchain-based self-sovereign identity can fix the internet's trust problem.
content:
## The Identity Problem

Your digital identity is scattered across hundreds of databases you don't control. Each one is a breach waiting to happen. Each one is a single point of failure.

Centralized identity providers are:
- **Honeypots**: Massive data stores attracting attackers
- **Gatekeepers**: They decide who gets access
- **Surveillance tools**: They track your behavior across services

## Self-Sovereign Identity (SSI)

SSI puts you in control:

- **You own your credentials**: Stored in your wallet, not someone else's database
- **Selective disclosure**: Share only what's needed — prove you're over 18 without revealing your birthdate
- **Portable**: Works across platforms without vendor lock-in
- **Revocable**: You decide who has access, and for how long

## The Technology Stack

1. **DIDs (Decentralized Identifiers)**: Unique identifiers anchored on-chain
2. **Verifiable Credentials**: Digitally signed claims about your attributes
3. **ZK Proofs**: Prove claims without revealing underlying data

## Our Approach: Phantom ID

Phantom ID combines all three layers:

```
User → Creates DID → Receives VCs → Generates ZK Proofs → Verifier
                                          ↑
                              No personal data revealed
```

The result: trustless verification that respects privacy.

## Why Blockchain?

Blockchain provides the trust layer that SSI needs:
- Immutable credential registry
- Decentralized revocation checks
- No single point of failure
- Censorship resistant

The future of identity isn't about who you are. It's about what you can prove.

title: The Future of On-Device AI: Why the Cloud is the Enemy
excerpt: Every API call is a privacy leak. On-device inference isn't just about latency — it's about sovereignty over your own computation.
content:
## The Cloud Problem

Every time you send a prompt to a cloud LLM, you're shipping your thoughts to someone else's computer. Your queries, your context, your intent — all logged, all stored, all monetizable.

The cloud isn't just a convenience. It's a **surveillance vector**.

## Why On-Device Matters

On-device AI flips the model:

- **Zero network dependency**: Works offline, works in censored regions
- **No data leaves your device**: Your prompts stay yours
- **Predictable latency**: No cold starts, no rate limits, no outages
- **True ownership**: You own the model, the weights, the inference

## The Technical Challenge

Running LLMs locally isn't trivial. The constraints are real:

- **Memory**: A 7B parameter model needs ~4GB in 4-bit quantization
- **Compute**: GPU acceleration is essential for usable speeds
- **Quality**: Smaller models sacrifice capability for efficiency

## Our Approach: Spectra AI

Spectra AI tackles these constraints head-on:

```
Model → Quantize (4-bit) → Optimize (ONNX) → Deploy (WebGPU)
                                                    ↓
                                          Browser / Desktop / Mobile
```

### Key innovations:
- **Adaptive quantization**: Different precision for attention vs. feed-forward layers
- **Speculative decoding**: Use a tiny draft model to predict tokens, verify with the main model
- **KV cache optimization**: Sliding window attention to bound memory usage

## Benchmarks

On an M2 MacBook Air:
- 7B model: ~30 tokens/sec
- 3B model: ~60 tokens/sec
- 1B model: ~120 tokens/sec

For most tasks — summarization, code completion, analysis — the 3B model is more than sufficient.

## The Privacy Guarantee

With Spectra AI, the privacy story is simple: **there is no server**. No API key. No terms of service. No data processing agreement needed.

Your model. Your hardware. Your data. Period.

## What's Next

We're working on:
- **Model fine-tuning on device**: Personalize without uploading data
- **Encrypted model sharing**: Share fine-tuned models without exposing training data
- **Multi-model orchestration**: Route tasks to the right local model automatically

The future of AI is personal. And personal means private.

title: Mixer Networks and the War on Metadata
excerpt: Encryption protects content. Mixnets protect context. Why metadata resistance is the next frontier in privacy engineering.
content:
## The Metadata Problem

You encrypted your message. Great. But the network still knows:

- **Who** you're talking to
- **When** you're talking
- **How often** you communicate
- **How much** data you're sending
- **Where** you are physically located

This is metadata, and it's often more revealing than content.

> "We kill people based on metadata." — Former NSA Director Michael Hayden

## What Mixnets Solve

A mixnet (mix network) routes messages through a series of nodes that:

1. **Collect** messages from multiple senders
2. **Shuffle** the order randomly
3. **Delay** messages by random intervals
4. **Strip** identifying information
5. **Forward** to the next mix node

The result: even if an adversary controls some nodes, they can't link senders to recipients.

## Mixnets vs. Tor

| Feature | Tor | Mixnet |
|---------|-----|--------|
| Latency | Low (~100ms) | High (~seconds) |
| Traffic analysis resistance | Weak | Strong |
| Timing correlation | Vulnerable | Resistant |
| Cover traffic | None | Yes |
| Best for | Browsing | Messaging |

Tor is fast but vulnerable to traffic analysis. Mixnets sacrifice speed for stronger metadata protection.

## The Architecture

```
Sender → Mix Node 1 → Mix Node 2 → Mix Node 3 → Recipient
              ↑             ↑             ↑
         Shuffle +      Shuffle +     Shuffle +
         Delay +        Delay +       Delay +
         Decrypt        Decrypt       Decrypt
```

Each layer of encryption is peeled off at each node — similar to Tor, but with the critical addition of **shuffling** and **timing delays**.

## Cover Traffic

The secret weapon of mixnets is **cover traffic** — fake messages that pad real communication:

- Prevents traffic volume analysis
- Makes it impossible to determine if someone is actually communicating
- Constant bandwidth usage regardless of activity

## Implementation Challenges

- **Latency**: Users expect instant messaging. Multi-second delays feel broken
- **Bandwidth**: Cover traffic is expensive at scale
- **Reliability**: More hops = more failure points
- **Incentives**: Who runs mix nodes, and why?

## Our Approach

GhostNet VPN integrates mixnet principles with practical usability:

- **Adaptive mixing**: Stronger mixing for messaging, lighter for browsing
- **Incentivized nodes**: Community-run nodes earn tokens for reliable operation
- **Hybrid routing**: Use Tor for low-latency, mixnet for high-security

## The Bottom Line

Encryption is necessary but not sufficient. If you care about privacy, you need to care about metadata. Mixnets are how we get there.

The next generation of privacy tools won't just encrypt your words. They'll hide the fact that you spoke at all.

title: Smart Contract Security: Lessons from $3B in DeFi Exploits
excerpt: Every major DeFi exploit shares common patterns. Here's what we've learned from auditing contracts and how to build defensively.
content:
## The Attack Surface

Smart contracts are **immutable** and **transparent**. Once deployed, bugs become permanent and visible to every attacker on the planet. This is both the strength and weakness of blockchain.

In 2025 alone, DeFi protocols lost over $1.2B to exploits. Most shared common vulnerability patterns.

## The Top 5 Vulnerability Classes

### 1. Reentrancy
The classic. A contract calls an external address before updating state, allowing recursive calls.

```
// VULNERABLE
function withdraw(uint amount) {
    require(balances[msg.sender] >= amount);
    (bool success, ) = msg.sender.call{value: amount}("");
    require(success);
    balances[msg.sender] -= amount;  // Too late
}
```

**Fix**: Checks-effects-interactions pattern. Update state before external calls.

### 2. Oracle Manipulation
Protocols that rely on spot prices from DEX pools are trivially exploitable via flash loans.

**Fix**: Use time-weighted average prices (TWAPs), multiple oracle sources, and circuit breakers.

### 3. Access Control Failures
Missing `onlyOwner` modifiers, unprotected `initialize()` functions, or misconfigured role permissions.

**Fix**: Use battle-tested access control libraries. Test every admin function.

### 4. Integer Overflow/Underflow
Less common since Solidity 0.8+ added built-in checks, but still appears in assembly blocks and unchecked math.

**Fix**: Avoid `unchecked` blocks unless you've proven correctness. Use SafeMath for older contracts.

### 5. Logic Errors
The hardest to catch — the code does what it says, but the developer's mental model was wrong.

**Fix**: Formal verification, extensive fuzzing, and multiple independent auditors.

## Our Audit Process

At NullShift, every audit follows a rigorous pipeline:

1. **Automated analysis**: Slither, Mythril, Echidna fuzzing
2. **Manual review**: Line-by-line code review by two independent auditors
3. **Invariant testing**: Define what should NEVER happen, then try to break it
4. **Economic modeling**: Simulate attacks with realistic flash loan scenarios
5. **Report**: Severity classification (Critical/High/Medium/Low/Info)

## Defensive Development Principles

- **Minimize attack surface**: Less code = fewer bugs. Simplicity is security
- **Fail closed**: When in doubt, revert the transaction
- **Rate limiting**: Implement cooldown periods for large operations
- **Emergency stops**: Circuit breakers for when things go wrong
- **Upgrade paths**: But upgradeable contracts add their own attack surface

## The Hard Truth

No audit guarantees security. Audits reduce risk, they don't eliminate it. The best defense is a combination of:

- Good architecture
- Comprehensive testing
- Independent audits
- Bug bounty programs
- Gradual deployment with TVL caps

Security isn't a destination. It's a process.

title: Federated Learning Without the Trust Problem
excerpt: Federated learning promised to keep data local. But without differential privacy and secure aggregation, the server still learns too much. Here's how to close the gap.
content:
## The Promise and the Lie

Federated learning (FL) was supposed to solve the privacy problem in machine learning. Instead of shipping raw data to a central server, each participant trains locally and only shares model updates — gradients or weight deltas. The server aggregates these updates into a global model. Data never leaves the device.

Sounds clean. The reality is messier.

Research has repeatedly shown that raw gradient updates leak private information. Attacks like Deep Leakage from Gradients (DLG) and its successors can reconstruct training images and text from shared gradients with alarming fidelity. The honest-but-curious server sees everything it needs to invert your local training data.

Federated learning without additional protections is privacy theater.

## Threat Model: Who Are We Defending Against?

Before bolting on defenses, define the adversary:

- **Honest-but-curious server**: Follows the protocol correctly but tries to extract private information from received updates
- **Malicious participants**: Submit poisoned updates to manipulate the global model (model poisoning) or inject backdoors
- **External eavesdropper**: Observes communication between clients and server
- **Colluding subsets**: A fraction of participants and/or the server conspire against the remaining honest parties

A robust FL system must handle all four. Most deployed systems handle zero.

## Differential Privacy: Bounding Information Leakage

Differential privacy (DP) provides a mathematical guarantee: the output of a computation changes negligibly whether or not any single individual's data is included. In FL, we apply DP at the local level before transmitting updates.

### Local Differential Privacy in FL

Each client clips its gradient update to a bounded L2 norm and adds calibrated Gaussian noise:

```python
def local_dp_update(gradients, clip_norm, noise_multiplier, lot_size):
    # Clip per-sample gradients
    for i in range(len(gradients)):
        grad_norm = torch.norm(gradients[i])
        clip_factor = min(1.0, clip_norm / (grad_norm + 1e-6))
        gradients[i] = gradients[i] * clip_factor

    # Aggregate clipped gradients
    avg_gradient = torch.mean(torch.stack(gradients), dim=0)

    # Add Gaussian noise calibrated to sensitivity
    sigma = noise_multiplier * clip_norm / lot_size
    noise = torch.randn_like(avg_gradient) * sigma
    return avg_gradient + noise
```

The privacy budget is tracked using Renyi Differential Privacy (RDP) accountants, which provide tighter composition bounds than naive (epsilon, delta)-DP. The `opacus` library by Meta implements this end-to-end for PyTorch.

### The Utility-Privacy Tradeoff

More noise means more privacy but less model accuracy. This is not a theoretical concern — it is the central engineering challenge. Key levers:

- **Clipping norm**: Too tight destroys signal. Too loose inflates sensitivity
- **Noise multiplier**: Directly controls the epsilon budget per round
- **Number of participants per round**: More participants means the noise averages out — the "amplification by subsampling" effect
- **Number of training rounds**: Each round consumes privacy budget. Fewer rounds = stronger guarantees

In practice, FL systems with thousands of participants per round can achieve single-digit epsilon while maintaining competitive accuracy. Systems with fewer than 100 participants per round struggle.

## Secure Aggregation: The Server Sees Nothing

Differential privacy bounds what the server can *learn*. Secure aggregation (SecAgg) ensures the server can't even *see* individual updates — only the aggregate.

### How SecAgg Works

The core idea is simple: mask each client's update so that masks cancel out in the sum.

1. **Pairwise key agreement**: Each pair of clients (i, j) agrees on a shared secret using Diffie-Hellman
2. **Mask generation**: Client i generates a pseudorandom mask for each peer using a PRG seeded with the shared secret
3. **Masked upload**: Client i uploads `update_i + sum(mask_ij for j > i) - sum(mask_ji for j < i)`
4. **Aggregation**: When the server sums all masked updates, pairwise masks cancel: `mask_ij + mask_ji = 0`
5. **Result**: The server obtains `sum(update_i)` without seeing any individual `update_i`

```python
# Simplified SecAgg mask generation
def generate_masks(client_id, peer_ids, shared_secrets, update_shape):
    mask = torch.zeros(update_shape)
    for peer_id in peer_ids:
        seed = shared_secrets[(client_id, peer_id)]
        prg = torch.Generator().manual_seed(seed)
        peer_mask = torch.randn(update_shape, generator=prg)
        if peer_id > client_id:
            mask += peer_mask
        else:
            mask -= peer_mask
    return mask
```

### Handling Dropouts

The critical complication: if a client drops out after committing to participate, the masks don't cancel. Google's original SecAgg protocol (Bonawitz et al., 2017) handles this with Shamir secret sharing — each client's secret key is split among peers so that a threshold can reconstruct the dropout's mask.

The communication cost is O(n^2) for n clients. SecAgg+ (Bell et al., 2020) reduces this to O(n log n) using sparse random graphs instead of a complete graph.

## Combining DP + SecAgg: Defense in Depth

These two mechanisms are complementary, not redundant:

- **SecAgg** prevents the server from seeing individual updates (input privacy)
- **DP** bounds what can be learned even from the aggregate (output privacy)

With both enabled, the server receives only a noisy aggregate. Even if SecAgg is broken (e.g., through collusion), DP still limits information leakage. Even if the DP noise is insufficient, SecAgg prevents direct gradient inspection.

```
Client: local_update = train_on_local_data()
Client: noised_update = add_dp_noise(local_update)  # DP layer
Client: masked_update = apply_secagg_mask(noised_update)  # SecAgg layer
Client: send(masked_update)

Server: aggregate = sum(masked_updates)  # Masks cancel
Server: global_model += aggregate / num_clients  # Only sees noisy aggregate
```

## Defending Against Malicious Participants

DP and SecAgg protect clients from the server. But what about malicious clients poisoning the model?

### Byzantine-Robust Aggregation

Replace naive averaging with robust aggregation rules:

- **Trimmed mean**: Discard the top and bottom k% of values per coordinate before averaging
- **Krum**: Select the update closest to its neighbors (Byzantine-tolerant selection)
- **Geometric median**: Compute the geometric median of updates (robust to up to 50% corruption)
- **FLTrust**: Server maintains a small clean dataset, uses it to compute trust scores for client updates

### The Tension with SecAgg

Here is the fundamental problem: robust aggregation requires inspecting individual updates. SecAgg hides individual updates. These goals directly conflict.

Recent work on **verifiable computation** and **zero-knowledge proofs** offers a way out. Clients can prove in zero knowledge that their update satisfies certain properties (bounded norm, consistency with a valid local training run) without revealing the update itself.

This is an active research frontier. At NullShift, we are building prototype circuits for ZK-verified FL updates using the Halo2 proving system.

## Practical Deployment Architecture

Our FL pipeline for Spectra AI's on-device model fine-tuning:

```
┌─────────────┐     ┌──────────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐
│  Client 1   │     │   Aggregation    │     │  Global     │
│  Local Train├────►│   Server         ├────►│  Model      │
│  + DP Noise │     │   (SecAgg only)  │     │  Update     │
│  + SecAgg   │     │                  │     │             │
└─────────────┘     │  Receives only   │     └─────────────┘
┌─────────────┐     │  noisy aggregate │
│  Client 2   ├────►│                  │
│  ...        │     │  Robust filtering│
└─────────────┘     │  on aggregate    │
┌─────────────┐     │  statistics      │
│  Client N   ├────►│                  │
│             │     └──────────────────┘
└─────────────┘
```

### Configuration parameters:

- **Min clients per round**: 500 (for amplification by subsampling)
- **Clipping norm**: 1.0 (tuned via sensitivity analysis)
- **Noise multiplier**: 0.8 (targets epsilon ~4 over 100 rounds)
- **SecAgg threshold**: 70% of committed clients must complete the round
- **Dropout tolerance**: Up to 30% via Shamir secret sharing (t=0.7n)

## Privacy Accounting: Tracking the Budget

Every round of FL consumes privacy budget. Once the budget is exhausted, the system must stop training — or accept that privacy guarantees have degraded.

We use the PRV (Privacy loss Random Variable) accountant, which provides the tightest known composition bounds:

```python
from dp_accounting.pld import privacy_loss_distribution

def compute_epsilon(noise_multiplier, sample_rate, num_rounds, delta=1e-6):
    pld = privacy_loss_distribution.from_gaussian_mechanism(
        standard_deviation=noise_multiplier,
        sensitivity=1.0,
        sampling_probability=sample_rate
    )
    composed = pld.self_compose(num_rounds)
    return composed.get_epsilon_for_delta(delta)
```

For our deployment parameters (noise_multiplier=0.8, sample_rate=500/100000, 100 rounds), this yields epsilon approximately 3.8 at delta=1e-6. This is a meaningful privacy guarantee — it means no single participant's contribution can be distinguished with more than ~98% confidence.

## The Road Ahead

Federated learning is not a solved problem. The open challenges are significant:

- **Communication efficiency**: Gradient compression and quantization to reduce bandwidth
- **Heterogeneity**: Clients have wildly different data distributions (non-IID problem)
- **Fairness**: The global model may perform poorly for minority data distributions
- **Verification**: Proving that the server actually performed honest aggregation
- **Incentives**: Why should a client spend compute to improve a shared model?

But the trajectory is clear. The combination of federated learning, differential privacy, and secure aggregation gives us a framework where model training does not require data centralization. The trust problem doesn't disappear — but it shrinks to a manageable surface.

At NullShift, we are not waiting for the research to converge. We are building now, deploying now, and iterating in production. Because the alternative — shipping your data to someone else's GPU cluster — is not acceptable.

```
nullshift@labs$ echo "Your data trains your model. Nobody else's."
Your data trains your model. Nobody else's.
nullshift@labs$ _
```

Keep your gradients. Keep your privacy. Build federated.

title: Building a Privacy-First DNS Resolver
excerpt: DNS is the internet's unencrypted address book. NullDNS encrypts every lookup, blocks trackers at the resolver level, and keeps zero logs.
content:
## DNS: The Privacy Hole Nobody Talks About

You installed a VPN. You use HTTPS everywhere. You run a privacy browser. But every time you visit a website, your DNS query goes out in plaintext — broadcasting your browsing intent to your ISP, your network operator, and anyone listening on the wire.

Traditional DNS (port 53, UDP) is unencrypted, unauthenticated, and trivially interceptable. It is the single largest metadata leak in most people's internet stack.

## The DNS Privacy Stack

Two protocols have emerged to fix this:

### DNS over HTTPS (DoH) — RFC 8484

DNS queries are wrapped inside standard HTTPS requests to port 443. From a network perspective, DoH traffic is indistinguishable from normal web browsing. This makes it extremely difficult for ISPs and firewalls to selectively block or inspect DNS traffic.

```
# DoH query using curl
curl -s -H 'Accept: application/dns-json' \
  'https://dns.nullshift.sh/dns-query?name=example.com&type=A'

# Response
{
  "Status": 0,
  "Answer": [
    {
      "name": "example.com",
      "type": 1,
      "TTL": 300,
      "data": "93.184.216.34"
    }
  ]
}
```

### DNS over TLS (DoT) — RFC 7858

DNS queries are sent over a dedicated TLS connection on port 853. Simpler to implement than DoH and provides the same encryption guarantees, but the dedicated port makes it easy for network operators to identify and block DoT traffic.

### Which One?

| Feature | DoH | DoT |
|---------|-----|-----|
| Port | 443 (blends with HTTPS) | 853 (dedicated) |
| Blockable | Very difficult | Trivial |
| Inspection resistance | High | Low |
| Implementation | More complex | Simpler |
| Browser support | Native in Firefox, Chrome | OS-level only |

NullDNS supports both. For users in censored networks, DoH is the default because it is indistinguishable from normal web traffic.

## Tracker Blocking at the DNS Level

Encryption is only half the story. The second function of NullDNS is aggressive tracker and ad blocking at the resolver level.

When a DNS query arrives for a known tracker domain, NullDNS returns `0.0.0.0` (NXDOMAIN for the client) instead of the real IP. The tracker never loads. No connection is established. No fingerprinting scripts execute.

### The Blocklist Pipeline

NullDNS maintains a curated blocklist compiled from multiple sources:

- **Community lists**: EasyList, EasyPrivacy, Steven Black's hosts
- **Proprietary intelligence**: Domains identified through traffic analysis of known tracking SDKs
- **Real-time classification**: New domains are analyzed using heuristic rules and ML-based domain generation algorithm (DGA) detection

```python
# NullDNS blocklist matching (simplified)
class BlocklistEngine:
    def __init__(self):
        self.exact_match = set()      # O(1) lookup
        self.suffix_match = []         # *.tracker.com patterns
        self.regex_patterns = []       # Complex patterns

    def load_lists(self, sources):
        for source in sources:
            rules = fetch_and_parse(source)
            for rule in rules:
                if rule.type == 'exact':
                    self.exact_match.add(rule.domain)
                elif rule.type == 'suffix':
                    self.suffix_match.append(rule.pattern)
                elif rule.type == 'regex':
                    self.regex_patterns.append(re.compile(rule.pattern))

    def should_block(self, domain):
        if domain in self.exact_match:
            return True
        for suffix in self.suffix_match:
            if domain.endswith(suffix):
                return True
        for pattern in self.regex_patterns:
            if pattern.match(domain):
                return True
        return False
```

The blocklist currently contains over 380,000 domains. It is updated every 6 hours.

## Zero-Log Architecture

Encryption protects the query in transit. Blocking stops trackers from loading. But if the resolver itself keeps logs, you have just moved the surveillance point from the ISP to the DNS provider.

NullDNS operates a **zero-log architecture**:

- **No query logs**: We do not record which domains you look up
- **No IP logs**: We do not record your source IP address
- **No session logs**: We do not correlate queries across time
- **Ephemeral cache only**: DNS responses are cached in memory for TTL duration, then discarded. No disk writes

### How We Enforce This

Promises are not enough. NullDNS is designed so that logging is architecturally infeasible:

- The resolver process runs with no write permissions to any persistent storage
- Memory is allocated from a fixed-size pool that is zeroed on process restart
- The query path contains no serialization step — there is no point where a query could be written to a structured log
- Independent security auditors verify the architecture quarterly

```
nullshift@labs$ nulldns --config /etc/nulldns/config.toml
[INFO] Loading blocklist: 382,419 domains
[INFO] Starting DoH listener on 0.0.0.0:443
[INFO] Starting DoT listener on 0.0.0.0:853
[INFO] Cache: 256MB ephemeral (no disk backing)
[INFO] Logging: DISABLED (no write permissions)
[INFO] NullDNS ready. Zero queries recorded.
```

## DNSSEC Validation

NullDNS validates DNSSEC signatures on every response when available. This prevents DNS spoofing attacks where an adversary injects forged DNS responses to redirect users to malicious servers.

The validation chain works as follows:

1. Query the authoritative nameserver for the requested domain
2. Retrieve the RRSIG (resource record signature) for the response
3. Walk the chain of trust from the domain's DNSKEY up through DS records to the root zone
4. Verify each signature in the chain using the public keys
5. If any signature is invalid or missing, mark the response as BOGUS and return SERVFAIL

DNSSEC does not provide privacy (queries are still plaintext to the authoritative server), but combined with DoH/DoT it provides both confidentiality and integrity.

## Encrypted Client Hello (ECH) Integration

Even with encrypted DNS, a network observer can still see the Server Name Indication (SNI) in the TLS handshake when you connect to a website. This leaks the hostname you are visiting.

NullDNS publishes ECH (Encrypted Client Hello) keys via DNS HTTPS records (SVCB/TYPE65), enabling supporting browsers to encrypt the SNI field. Combined with DoH, this closes the last major plaintext metadata leak in the HTTPS connection flow.

```
# ECH key published via DNS
_https._tcp.example.com. IN SVCB 1 . (
    ech="AEX+DQBBBQAgACBg0Nh0AE..."
    alpn="h2,h3"
)
```

## Performance: Latency Matters

A privacy-first DNS resolver is useless if it adds 200ms to every page load. NullDNS maintains performance through:

- **Anycast deployment**: Servers in 12 global locations via Railway and Fly.io edge nodes
- **Aggressive caching**: Popular domains are prefetched before TTL expiration
- **Connection reuse**: DoH uses HTTP/2 multiplexing, DoT uses persistent TLS sessions
- **QUIC support**: DoH3 (DNS over HTTP/3 / QUIC) for 0-RTT connection establishment

Median resolution time: **12ms** from nearest edge node. 95th percentile: **38ms**. This is faster than most ISP resolvers, which typically range 20-80ms.

## Setting Up NullDNS

For individual users, the fastest path is to point your system DNS to NullDNS:

```
# macOS
networksetup -setdnsservers Wi-Fi 100.64.0.1 100.64.0.2

# Linux (systemd-resolved)
[Resolve]
DNS=100.64.0.1#dns.nullshift.sh
DNSOverTLS=yes

# Firefox (DoH)
Settings → Privacy & Security → DNS over HTTPS → Custom
https://dns.nullshift.sh/dns-query
```

For organizations and power users, NullDNS can be self-hosted. The resolver binary is a single static executable with zero runtime dependencies:

```bash
nullshift@labs$ curl -sL https://get.nullshift.sh/nulldns | sh
nullshift@labs$ nulldns init --doh --dot --blocklist=standard
nullshift@labs$ nulldns run
```

## The Bigger Picture

DNS is just one layer. A truly private internet stack requires encrypted DNS, encrypted transport (TLS 1.3 + ECH), metadata-resistant routing (mixnets or Tor), and zero-knowledge authentication. Each layer closes a different leak.

NullDNS closes the first one. The foundation layer. Because if your address lookups are visible, nothing else you encrypt matters.

```
nullshift@labs$ dig +short privacy.nullshift.sh
> Encrypted. Blocked. Forgotten.
nullshift@labs$ _
```

Your DNS queries are your browsing history in plaintext. Stop leaking them.

title: Cross-Chain Bridges: Attack Vectors and Defenses
excerpt: Bridges are the weakest link in multi-chain DeFi. From validator compromises to replay attacks, here's a field guide to bridge security — and how to build bridges that don't burn.
content:
## Bridges: The Necessary Evil

Blockchains are islands. Each chain has its own consensus, its own state, its own security model. Cross-chain bridges connect these islands, allowing assets and messages to move between them. In doing so, they inherit the security assumptions of *every chain they connect* — plus introduce entirely new attack surfaces of their own.

Bridges hold enormous value. The Ronin bridge held $625M before it was drained. Wormhole held $320M before its exploit. Nomad held $190M before its collapse. Bridges are honeypots by construction: they custody assets on behalf of users across multiple chains.

The question is not whether bridges get attacked. It is how to build them so that attacks fail.

## Bridge Architecture Taxonomy

Before analyzing attacks, understand the three fundamental bridge designs:

### 1. Externally Verified (Multisig / Validator Set)

A committee of validators attests to events on the source chain and signs messages authorizing actions on the destination chain.

```
Source Chain: User locks 10 ETH in bridge contract
      ↓
Validator Set: 5-of-8 validators sign attestation
      ↓
Destination Chain: Bridge contract mints 10 wETH
```

**Trust assumption**: Honest majority of validators. If the threshold is compromised, funds are stolen.

**Examples**: Ronin (Axie Infinity), Multichain, early Wormhole

### 2. Optimistically Verified

Anyone can submit a message claiming an event occurred on the source chain. There is a challenge period during which watchers can submit fraud proofs to dispute invalid messages.

**Trust assumption**: At least one honest watcher is online during the challenge period.

**Examples**: Nomad, Optimism's canonical bridge, Across Protocol

### 3. Natively Verified (Light Client)

The destination chain runs a light client of the source chain, verifying block headers and Merkle proofs directly. No external validator set needed.

**Trust assumption**: Same as the source chain's consensus. The strongest model.

**Examples**: IBC (Cosmos), zkBridge (uses ZK proofs of consensus)

## Attack Vector 1: Validator Key Compromise

The most common and most devastating attack. If an attacker obtains enough validator private keys to meet the signing threshold, they can authorize arbitrary withdrawals.

### Case Study: Ronin Bridge ($625M, March 2022)

Ronin used a 5-of-9 validator multisig. The Lazarus Group (North Korean state-sponsored) compromised 5 validators through a combination of:

- Social engineering (fake job offer to a senior engineer)
- Compromised third-party infrastructure (the Axie DAO validator was still authorized from a temporary gas-free transaction arrangement)

With 5 keys, the attacker signed fraudulent withdrawal messages and drained 173,600 ETH + 25.5M USDC. The exploit went undetected for 6 days.

### Defenses

- **Higher thresholds**: 7-of-10 instead of 5-of-9 makes compromise exponentially harder
- **Key rotation**: Rotate validator keys on a fixed schedule. Compromised keys have a limited window
- **Hardware security modules (HSMs)**: Keys stored in tamper-resistant hardware, never exposed to general-purpose compute
- **Geographic and organizational diversity**: Validators in different jurisdictions, run by different entities
- **Time-locked withdrawals**: Large withdrawals require a delay period, giving watchers time to flag anomalies

```solidity
// Time-locked withdrawal pattern
mapping(bytes32 => uint256) public withdrawalTimestamps;
uint256 public constant WITHDRAWAL_DELAY = 24 hours;
uint256 public constant LARGE_WITHDRAWAL_THRESHOLD = 1000 ether;

function initiateWithdrawal(bytes32 txHash, uint256 amount, address to) external onlyValidators {
    if (amount >= LARGE_WITHDRAWAL_THRESHOLD) {
        withdrawalTimestamps[txHash] = block.timestamp;
        emit WithdrawalQueued(txHash, amount, to, block.timestamp + WITHDRAWAL_DELAY);
    } else {
        _executeWithdrawal(txHash, amount, to);
    }
}

function finalizeWithdrawal(bytes32 txHash, uint256 amount, address to) external {
    require(withdrawalTimestamps[txHash] != 0, "Not queued");
    require(block.timestamp >= withdrawalTimestamps[txHash] + WITHDRAWAL_DELAY, "Too early");
    _executeWithdrawal(txHash, amount, to);
}
```

## Attack Vector 2: Smart Contract Vulnerabilities

The bridge contract itself may contain bugs that allow unauthorized minting or withdrawal.

### Case Study: Wormhole ($320M, February 2022)

Wormhole's Solana-side contract had a signature verification bypass. The attacker exploited a deprecated `verify_signatures` instruction that did not properly validate the guardian set. They forged a fake VAA (Verified Action Approval) and minted 120,000 wETH on Solana without any deposit on Ethereum.

The root cause was a single missing check in the signature verification path — the contract accepted a `SignatureSet` account without verifying it was produced by the legitimate verification program.

### Defenses

- **Formal verification**: Mathematically prove critical invariants (total minted <= total deposited)
- **Multiple independent audits**: Different firms catch different classes of bugs
- **Invariant monitoring**: On-chain monitoring that pauses the bridge if invariants are violated
- **Upgradeable contracts with timelocks**: Fix bugs, but with governance delays to prevent malicious upgrades

## Attack Vector 3: Replay Attacks

A valid message on one chain is replayed on another to double-spend.

### The Mechanism

If a bridge does not include chain-specific identifiers in its message format, a legitimate deposit message on Chain A can be replayed on Chain B to claim tokens a second time.

```
// VULNERABLE message format (no chain ID)
message = encode(sender, recipient, amount, nonce)

// SECURE message format
message = encode(source_chain_id, dest_chain_id, sender, recipient, amount, nonce, bridge_address)
```

### Case Study: Nomad Bridge ($190M, August 2022)

Nomad's exploit was even worse than a replay — it was a *proof bypass*. A routine upgrade initialized the trusted root to `0x00`, which meant the `prove()` function accepted any message as valid. Once the first attacker demonstrated the exploit, hundreds of copycats replayed the same transaction pattern with different addresses. It became a free-for-all.

### Defenses

- **Chain-specific message encoding**: Always include source chain ID, destination chain ID, and contract address in signed messages
- **Nonce tracking**: Track processed nonces per source chain to prevent duplicate processing
- **Domain separation**: Use EIP-712 structured data hashing with chain-specific domain separators

```solidity
// EIP-712 domain separator for bridge messages
bytes32 public DOMAIN_SEPARATOR = keccak256(abi.encode(
    keccak256("EIP712Domain(string name,string version,uint256 chainId,address verifyingContract)"),
    keccak256("NullBridge"),
    keccak256("1"),
    block.chainid,
    address(this)
));
```

## Attack Vector 4: Governance Attacks

If the bridge's upgrade mechanism is controlled by a governance token, an attacker can acquire enough tokens (potentially via flash loan) to push through a malicious upgrade.

### Defenses

- **Timelock on all upgrades**: Minimum 48-hour delay between proposal and execution
- **Flash loan protection**: Snapshot voting power at a past block height
- **Guardian veto**: A security council can veto malicious proposals during the timelock period
- **Immutable core logic**: Make the critical verification and custody code non-upgradeable

## Attack Vector 5: Censorship and Liveness Failures

If the validator set goes offline or refuses to process messages, user funds are stuck. This is not theft, but it breaks the bridge's fundamental guarantee.

### Defenses

- **Escape hatch**: After a timeout period (e.g., 7 days of bridge inactivity), users can withdraw directly from the source chain custody contract by proving their deposit
- **Decentralized validator incentives**: Economic penalties (slashing) for validator downtime
- **Fallback relayer network**: Multiple independent relayer implementations to prevent single points of failure

## The NullBridge Architecture

At NullShift, we are building NullBridge with a layered verification approach:

```
Layer 1: ZK Light Client (primary verification)
  - Verify source chain consensus using ZK proofs of block headers
  - No external validator set needed for chains with finality

Layer 2: Optimistic Fallback (for chains without ZK support)
  - 4-hour challenge period
  - Bonded watchers with slashing for missed fraud proofs

Layer 3: Rate Limiting and Circuit Breakers
  - Per-asset, per-hour flow limits
  - Automatic pause if cumulative outflow exceeds 30% of TVL in 24 hours

Layer 4: Escape Hatch
  - 7-day timeout allows direct withdrawal from source chain
  - No trust assumption beyond source chain consensus
```

### The ZK Advantage

ZK-verified bridges are the endgame. Instead of trusting validators, the destination chain verifies a succinct proof that the source chain reached a specific state. The proof can verify:

- A block header is part of the canonical chain
- A specific transaction was included in that block
- The transaction has the claimed effects (deposit amount, recipient, etc.)

This reduces the trust assumption to the soundness of the proof system and the security of the source chain — no multisig, no validator set, no governance token.

## Monitoring: The Last Line of Defense

Every bridge should have real-time monitoring for:

- **TVL anomalies**: Sudden drops in locked value indicate an exploit in progress
- **Mint/burn imbalances**: Minted tokens on the destination should never exceed deposits on the source
- **Validator behavior**: Unusual signing patterns, keys signing from unexpected IP ranges
- **Governance proposals**: Automated alerts for any proposal that modifies bridge parameters

```python
# Simplified bridge monitor
class BridgeMonitor:
    def __init__(self, bridge_address, alert_webhook):
        self.bridge = bridge_address
        self.alert = alert_webhook
        self.last_tvl = self.get_tvl()

    def check_invariants(self):
        current_tvl = self.get_tvl()
        tvl_change = (current_tvl - self.last_tvl) / self.last_tvl

        if tvl_change < -0.10:  # 10% drop
            self.alert.send(f"CRITICAL: TVL dropped {tvl_change:.1%} in monitoring window")

        minted = self.get_total_minted()
        deposited = self.get_total_deposited()
        if minted > deposited * 1.001:  # 0.1% tolerance for rounding
            self.alert.send(f"CRITICAL: Mint/deposit imbalance: {minted} > {deposited}")

        self.last_tvl = current_tvl
```

## The Bottom Line

Bridges are the most dangerous components in the multi-chain ecosystem. Every bridge is a bet that its security model won't be broken before the next generation of verification technology arrives.

Build bridges with defense in depth. Assume every component will be attacked. Make exploitation expensive, detectable, and recoverable. And when ZK light clients are production-ready, migrate to them.

The bridge that survives is the bridge that assumes it will be attacked.

```
nullshift@labs$ echo "Trust the math. Verify the proof. Bridge with caution."
Trust the math. Verify the proof. Bridge with caution.
nullshift@labs$ _
```

title: Recursive SNARKs: Proofs That Verify Proofs
excerpt: What if a proof could verify another proof? Recursive SNARKs enable incrementally verifiable computation, constant-size proof chains, and the compression of arbitrary computation into a single check.
content:
## The Composition Problem

A standard SNARK proves that a specific computation was performed correctly. But what happens when the computation is too large to prove in a single step? Or when new computation is continuously appended — like a blockchain producing blocks indefinitely?

You could generate a separate proof for each step and ask the verifier to check them all. But that scales linearly: N steps require N proof verifications. For a blockchain with millions of blocks, this is impractical.

Recursive SNARKs solve this by allowing a proof to *verify another proof* as part of its own computation. The result is a single proof that attests to the correctness of an entire chain of computation, no matter how long.

## How Recursion Works

The core idea: the SNARK circuit itself contains a verifier for SNARKs. The prover generates proof P_n that attests to two things:

1. Step n of the computation was performed correctly
2. Proof P_{n-1} (attesting to all previous steps) is valid

```
P_0: "Step 0 is correct"
P_1: "Step 1 is correct AND P_0 is valid"
P_2: "Step 2 is correct AND P_1 is valid"
...
P_n: "Step n is correct AND P_{n-1} is valid"
```

The verifier only needs to check P_n. If P_n is valid, then by induction, all steps 0 through n are correct. One proof, constant size, regardless of n.

## The Technical Challenge: Verifier-in-Circuit

The catch is that SNARK verification involves expensive operations — elliptic curve pairings, field arithmetic, hash function evaluations. Expressing the SNARK verifier as an arithmetic circuit (which is what the prover operates over) is extremely costly.

### The Cycle of Curves Approach

The original solution (used by Coda/Mina Protocol) relies on a **cycle of elliptic curves**: two curves where the base field of each is the scalar field of the other.

- **Curve A** has base field F_p and scalar field F_q
- **Curve B** has base field F_q and scalar field F_p

A proof generated over Curve A can be efficiently verified in a circuit defined over Curve B, and vice versa. This avoids the enormous overhead of non-native field arithmetic.

The Pasta curves (Pallas and Vesta) were designed specifically for this purpose:

```
Pallas: y^2 = x^3 + 5  over F_p
  where p = 0x40000000000000000000000000000000224698fc094cf91b992d30ed00000001

Vesta: y^2 = x^3 + 5  over F_q
  where q = 0x40000000000000000000000000000000224698fc0994a8dd8c46eb2100000001

Critical property: p = |Vesta|  and  q = |Pallas|
```

### The Accumulation Scheme Approach

Nova (Setty, 2021) introduced a fundamentally different approach. Instead of fully verifying the previous proof inside the circuit, Nova uses an **accumulation scheme** (also called a folding scheme).

The idea: instead of verifying P_{n-1}, the circuit *accumulates* it into a running accumulator. Verification is deferred to the very end. Each step only performs a cheap accumulation operation, not a full verification.

```
Step 0: acc_0 = fold(initial, instance_0)
Step 1: acc_1 = fold(acc_0, instance_1)
Step 2: acc_2 = fold(acc_1, instance_2)
...
Step n: acc_n = fold(acc_{n-1}, instance_n)

Final verification: verify(acc_n)  // One full verification at the end
```

The folding operation for Nova's R1CS-based IVC is remarkably simple — it's essentially a random linear combination of two R1CS instances:

```python
def fold(acc, new_instance, challenge):
    # acc and new_instance are R1CS instances (A, B, C, Z, E, u)
    # challenge r is derived via Fiat-Shamir
    r = hash(acc, new_instance)

    folded = R1CSInstance(
        E = acc.E + r * cross_term + r^2 * new_instance.E,
        u = acc.u + r * new_instance.u,
        W = acc.W + r * new_instance.W,
        X = acc.X + r * new_instance.X
    )
    return folded
```

This reduces the per-step overhead from thousands of constraints (for in-circuit verification) to roughly **two group scalar multiplications and a hash**. The prover cost per step drops by orders of magnitude.

## IVC: Incrementally Verifiable Computation

Recursive SNARKs are the building block for **IVC (Incrementally Verifiable Computation)** — a model where a long-running computation can be verified at any point by checking a single proof.

Formally, an IVC scheme provides:

- **Prover**: Given state S_i, transition function F, and proof pi_i, produce (S_{i+1}, pi_{i+1}) where pi_{i+1} attests that S_{i+1} = F(F(...F(S_0)...)) for all steps
- **Verifier**: Given (S_n, pi_n), verify that S_n is the correct result of applying F n times to S_0

### IVC in Practice

```rust
// Nova-based IVC step function (conceptual Rust)
struct IVCStep {
    state: Vec<F>,      // Current computation state
    proof: NovaProof,    // Accumulated proof
}

impl IVCStep {
    fn step(&self, transition_fn: impl Fn(&[F]) -> Vec<F>) -> IVCStep {
        // 1. Apply the computation step
        let new_state = transition_fn(&self.state);

        // 2. Fold the new step into the running proof
        let new_proof = nova_fold(
            &self.proof,
            &self.state,
            &new_state,
            &transition_fn  // Circuit representation of F
        );

        IVCStep {
            state: new_state,
            proof: new_proof,
        }
    }

    fn verify(&self, initial_state: &[F]) -> bool {
        nova_verify(&self.proof, initial_state, &self.state)
    }
}
```

## Practical Applications

### 1. Blockchain State Compression (Mina Protocol)

Mina uses recursive SNARKs to compress the entire blockchain into a single ~22KB proof. New nodes do not need to download and replay the full chain history — they verify one proof and have cryptographic certainty of the current state.

```
Traditional blockchain: Download 500GB, replay all transactions
Mina: Download 22KB proof, verify in <1 second
```

This is the canonical application of recursive proof composition.

### 2. ZK Rollup Proof Aggregation

ZK rollups like zkSync and StarkNet generate proofs for batches of transactions. Recursive composition allows aggregating multiple batch proofs into a single proof that is posted to L1.

```
Batch 1: 1000 txns → Proof_1
Batch 2: 1000 txns → Proof_2
Batch 3: 1000 txns → Proof_3

Aggregated: Proof_agg verifies (Proof_1, Proof_2, Proof_3)
L1 verifies only Proof_agg → one on-chain verification for 3000 txns
```

This directly reduces L1 gas costs, which is the primary bottleneck for rollup economics.

### 3. Verifiable Machine Execution (zkVM)

A zkVM (zero-knowledge virtual machine) executes arbitrary programs and produces a proof that the execution was correct. Recursive composition allows proving long-running programs that would otherwise exceed circuit size limits.

RISC Zero, SP1, and Nexus all use recursive proof composition to handle programs with millions or billions of execution cycles:

```
Execution trace: 1 billion cycles
Segment 1: Cycles 0-1M → Proof_1
Segment 2: Cycles 1M-2M → Proof_2 (verifies Proof_1)
...
Segment 1000: Cycles 999M-1B → Proof_1000 (verifies Proof_999)

Final: Single proof attesting to 1B cycles of correct execution
```

### 4. Private Computation Chains

Recursive proofs enable multi-party computation chains where each participant extends the computation without seeing previous inputs:

- Alice computes f(x) and produces proof P_A
- Bob computes g(f(x)) and produces proof P_B that verifies P_A
- Carol computes h(g(f(x))) and produces proof P_C that verifies P_B

Each participant proves their step is correct and that the previous chain is valid, without learning the intermediate values.

## The Proof System Landscape

Different recursive proof systems make different tradeoffs:

| System | Approach | Per-step cost | Final proof size | Trust setup |
|--------|----------|---------------|------------------|-------------|
| Halo2 | Accumulation (IPA) | Low | ~10KB | None |
| Nova | Folding (R1CS) | Very low | ~10KB + SNARK | None |
| SuperNova | Folding (multi-instruction) | Very low | ~10KB + SNARK | None |
| Plonky2 | Recursive FRI | Medium | ~50KB | None |
| Groth16 recursion | Cycle of curves | High | ~200B | Trusted setup |

Nova and its extensions (SuperNova for non-uniform computation, HyperNova for higher-degree relations) represent the current state of the art for IVC. The folding approach is simply more efficient than full in-circuit verification.

## SuperNova: Non-Uniform IVC

Standard Nova requires the same transition function F at every step. SuperNova (Setty, 2022) generalizes this to support different functions at each step:

```
Step 0: Apply F_3 (selected at runtime)
Step 1: Apply F_1
Step 2: Apply F_7
...
```

This is critical for zkVMs, where each CPU cycle executes a different instruction. Without SuperNova, you would need to encode *all possible instructions* into a single circuit and use selector bits — massively inflating circuit size.

With SuperNova, each instruction type has its own optimized circuit, and the prover only pays for the circuit corresponding to the instruction actually executed.

## Implementation: Building with Nova

Microsoft's Nova implementation (Rust) provides the core primitives:

```rust
use nova_snark::{
    traits::{circuit::TrivialCircuit, snark::RelaxedR1CSSNARKTrait, Engine},
    PublicParams, RecursiveSNARK, CompressedSNARK,
};

// 1. Define your step circuit
struct MyStepCircuit {
    // Private inputs for this step
    input: Vec<F>,
}

impl StepCircuit<F> for MyStepCircuit {
    fn arity(&self) -> usize { 1 }  // Number of state elements

    fn synthesize(
        &self,
        cs: &mut ConstraintSystem<F>,
        z: &[AllocatedNum<F>],  // Current state
    ) -> Result<Vec<AllocatedNum<F>>, SynthesisError> {
        // Define the computation step as R1CS constraints
        let new_state = /* your computation */;
        Ok(vec![new_state])
    }
}

// 2. Generate public parameters (one-time setup)
let pp = PublicParams::setup(&circuit, &trivial_circuit, &*default_ck_hint(), &*default_ck_hint());

// 3. Create and extend the IVC proof
let mut recursive_snark = RecursiveSNARK::new(&pp, &circuit, &trivial_circuit, &z0_primary, &z0_secondary)?;

for step in steps {
    recursive_snark.prove_step(&pp, &step_circuit, &trivial_circuit)?;
}

// 4. Verify
recursive_snark.verify(&pp, num_steps, &z0_primary, &z0_secondary)?;

// 5. Compress to a succinct proof (optional, for on-chain verification)
let compressed = CompressedSNARK::prove(&pp, &pk, &recursive_snark)?;
compressed.verify(&pp, &vk, num_steps, &z0_primary, &z0_secondary)?;
```

## What We Are Building

At NullShift, we use recursive proof composition in two core systems:

**1. NullBridge**: ZK light client verification uses recursive proofs to maintain a verified chain of block headers. Each new block header proof recursively verifies the previous chain, allowing the destination chain to validate the source chain's state with a single constant-size proof.

**2. NullVM**: Our experimental zkVM for privacy-preserving smart contracts uses SuperNova-based IVC to prove arbitrary program execution. The recursion allows programs of any length to produce a fixed-size proof.

The endgame is a world where computation is verified, not re-executed. Where trust is mathematical, not institutional. Recursive SNARKs are the mechanism that makes this scale.

```
nullshift@labs$ echo "One proof. All steps. Zero trust required."
One proof. All steps. Zero trust required.
nullshift@labs$ _
```

Proofs that verify proofs. Turtles all the way down — but with a soundness guarantee.
